meta.note: Canonical deck data — now tracking a LIVE system (Phases 0–11 shipped; the 7-day soak and 5-day trial are the open windows). Standing gate: every phase completion and direction-changing edit updates this file in the same push.

cast.note: Six real repos run this system. Their names are changed for this deck; the telemetry, events, and command output shown are real.

projects.what: a production-planning app with real end users
the hub machine — dispatch queue, event stream, timers

crew.name: mentat
crew.status: Live on 4 projects, nightly. Drafted its first real proposals from fleet telemetry — e.g. “medic incident flapping: 48 events vs 4 distinct in 7d.” The human front doors /bugfix and /feature enter the same loop through write-ticket.
crew.tagline: Fleet telemetry and human /bugfix and /feature reports both become drafted proposals.
crew.headline: Design was never a loop: every feature started as a chat conversation, gated by whatever the human operator happened to notice.
crew.gate: proposal approved/denied in the daily dispatch; denied proposals are never re-proposed

skills.name: write-ticket
skills.source: shipyard/skills/write-ticket — the precursor polish-ticket assumes; mentat drafts through it, humans invoke it
skills.summary: Turn an ask into a ticket with acceptance criteria.
skills.detail: Reads the project's own conventions from a [write_ticket] config block (context files, ticket dir, type enum, point scale, commit scopes — never baked), resolves the next id, clarifies only genuinely-blocking ambiguities (default-and-record: lock sensible defaults into the decisions table for veto at review), and emits the ticket skeleton with an explicit Definition of Done. Precursor to polish-ticket. [Phase 13, D-L16/D-O11]

skills.name: /bugfix
skills.source: shipyard/skills/bugfix — human front door into the design loop
skills.summary: Reproduce and root-cause, then write the ticket.
skills.detail: Reproduce first (a failing test, reliable steps, or a captured signature — no ticket until the defect is pinned), the repro fails because of the bug, falsify rival causes, separate root from symptom, scope to the minimum diff. Then write-ticket with a bug scope: acceptance is the repro passing. The repro travels in the ticket; execute-ticket commits the failing test at build as the permanent regression test. [Phase 13, D-O12]

skills.name: /feature
skills.source: shipyard/skills/feature — human front door into the design loop
skills.summary: Clarify the ask and set a Definition of Done, then write the ticket.
skills.detail: Surface assumptions and verify one per candidate, confirm the unverified ones with the human, then set acceptance criteria as a checklist with Boundaries (always / ask-first / never). Uses the project's existing DoD convention. Then write-ticket with a feature scope: acceptance is the DoD met. [Phase 13]

skills.name: polish-ticket
skills.source: shipyard/skills/ — symlinked into each project’s .claude/skills/ (agents + humans load the same file)
skills.summary: Hardens a spec until a zero-context agent can build it unattended.
skills.detail: The A–H checklist survives unchanged: self-containment (exact files, units, ports, dated numbers with the command that produced them), orchestration protocol with the verbatim anti-cheating brief, per-phase gate assembly, toolchain proof (§C2: never hand over a ticket whose verification method you haven't run while polishing), verification surfaces where a test that can't fail is a finding, decisions split locked / open-with-default / user-class, thin-slice phasing, ledger + observable DoD. What changes: the gate menu, dashboard port, notify path, and sibling-repo hazards stop being baked prose and become parameters from .agents/config.toml + a hub gate file. Callers: mentat headless after a proposal is stamped; the human operator in-session. [Phase 10, D-L11]

skills.name: telemetry collectors
skills.summary: Read-only signal mining across the fleet.
skills.detail: Sources verified live 2026-07-21: caddy access logs via journald (caddy logs to stdout — /var/log/caddy/ is empty), the event stream (92 day-files; pipe through jq -R 'fromjson?' — 2 corrupt lines abort bare jq), fyi-requests.jsonl, medic incident files, session transcripts under ~/.claude/projects/. Plus pilot usage beacons on salusa + caladan: POST /api/usage appending {ts, path, action, session} JSONL to gitignored data/usage/ — no third-party analytics, nothing leaves the box/tailnet. Further instrumentation ships as mentat's own proposals: the loop bootstraps itself. [D-L12, D-O8]

skills.name: proposal drafting
skills.summary: Nightly: signals → ≤3 open proposals per project.
skills.detail: Writes <project>/tmp/<project>-mentat-result.json (mirrors helldiver's result shape so the dispatch queue enrichment stays symmetric), emits design.proposal.opened events, respects a 1M-token daily cap. Skips drafting when 3 proposals sit undecided. Approve in the dispatch → mentat hardens via polish-ticket into <project>/docs/tickets/ and hands to helldiver. Deny → recorded in decisions.jsonl, never re-proposed. [D-O7, D-O9, D-L13] Incident-repair proposals (from suk) emit immediately, outside the nightly cadence, against the same token cap. [D-L15]

skills.name: coverage-audit
skills.source: shipyard/skills/coverage-audit — moved off user scope, symlinked per project
skills.summary: Session transcripts as ground truth for what automation misses.
skills.detail: Every time the user pointed out a bug in a session, the test suite had missed it. This skill samples the N most-recent transcripts, finds those moments, categorizes the failure classes, and triages each fix as project-specific / generic / install-time-configurable. It resolves the core ambiguity: 0 catches can mean 'stable' or 'checking what doesn't break'. Today it's trapped at user scope on one box; adapted, it installs with the system, renamed to role IDs, and its output feeds both mentat proposals and proctor rubric lines. [shared design + release]

crew.name: helldiver
crew.status: Live. Merge is opt-in and a zero-CI repo can’t merge vacuously; incident repairs no longer slip in a side-door — they enter as proposals at the top of the loop.
crew.tagline: Takes ratified tickets to shipped code.
crew.headline: Auto-merge was opt-out (default true), and a repo with zero CI checks passed the CI gate vacuously.
crew.gate: CI green + explicit opt-in merge; no checks = red (waivable only by allow_no_ci)

skills.name: execute-ticket
skills.source: shipyard/skills/ — symlinked per project; a default-off [build] ticket_mode hook lets the runner call it headless
skills.summary: Orchestrated build with mandatory on-system verification.
skills.detail: Portable core: discover-state step 0, green-baseline-before-touching-anything, thin vertical slices with a safety patch before risky changes, verify-before-commit (never trust a subagent's green — re-run and read the actual output), the honest-blocker protocol (revert to last green + precise report beats faked green), and the user-decision class as the only reason to stop (money, outward-facing, destructive, overriding deliberate config, real design forks) with a Signal alert when stopping. Parameterized: gate menu, notify command, event-stream path. Callers: helldiver headless for ratified tickets; the human operator in-session. [Phase 10, D-L11]

skills.name: feedback triage → PRs
skills.detail: The flow stays; the gates change. Fixed (1c): augur_can_merge default flips true→false at runner.sh:226 + medic/runner.sh:84 — merge becomes opt-in per project. Fixed (1d): the green|no_checks case at runner.sh:441 splits — no_checks fails the gate unless [augur] allow_no_ci = true, and a waiver emits augur.incident.ci_waived. The hot case that forced the ordering: salusa runs augur_can_merge = true with zero CI workflows, so its CI must exist (Phase 2) before these gates go live (Phase 4).

skills.name: incident repairs — via the loop
skills.source: retired — agents/build/incident-role.md removed (D-L15); incidents now route through the design loop
skills.summary: The incident side-door into build is retired.
skills.detail: How it worked before: suk's scan opened an incident and helldiver's incident-role.md drafted a fix PR directly — a second road into build that bypassed the design gate. Now (D-L15, live): suk keeps ungated mitigation (restart, revert, contain — on-call waits for no stamp), but the code fix is an incident-repair proposal emitted the moment the incident opens, paged to the operator, stamped in the dispatch, polished by mentat, built by helldiver, gated by proctor. incident-role.md is deleted and build --mode incident is retired. One road into build; repeated incidents become design signal instead of one-off patches.

crew.name: proctor
crew.status: Live. The shoulder-mode critic caught a planted auth-bypass on a pilot repo and delivered it into the session — the first real regression this loop has ever surfaced.
crew.tagline: Cold-context release-readiness critic; judges diffs while the code is being written.
crew.headline: Nightly re-runs of already-green tests catch nothing — the authoring session already optimized against that exact signal.
crew.gate: block findings must be addressed before ship; delivered as in-session notes, never hard stops (D-L2)

skills.name: shoulder-mode critic
skills.source: agents/release/critic-role.md — built Phase 6, piloted on caladan
skills.summary: Concurrent cold-context critique of the live diff.
skills.detail: Input is ONLY the diff, the changed-file list, the project's .agents/release.md, and the rubric — explicitly never the dev session transcript, because goal contamination is the thing this design exists to avoid. Debounce: 5 min idle OR 8 touched files (D-L3). Findings graded block / warn / note and delivered into the live session via claude-note.sh (exit 2 = ambiguous target, exit 3 = session at a prompt — retry, never treat as delivered). Budget: 1M tokens/day, enforced by summing today's critique events' token counts before spawning (D-O1 — a token cap, not dollars). It never writes code (D-L7). Verified by planting a real authz bug + an untested behavior change and requiring a block + warn within one debounce window.

skills.name: release-readiness rubric
skills.source: critic-role.md, tuned per project in .agents/<role>.md (+ a Conventions block for operator taste)
skills.summary: What 'ready to ship' means, written down.
skills.detail: v1 lines: does the change do what the task claims beyond the eval the dev agent optimized (Goodhart check); changed behavior covered by a changed/added test, deleted or weakened tests flagged; security boundaries (authz on new endpoints, input validation, secrets, injection); reversibility (migrations with down paths, no irreversible actions); blast radius (unexplained files = scope creep); observability of new failure modes; suppressions (eslint-disable, @ts-ignore, skipped tests) as explicit risk decisions. After a 2-week measure, lines that only ever produce notes get pruned. The rubric is also where the operator's conventions live: .agents/release.md carries a Conventions block of stated taste — fewest LOC possible, no new dependencies without justification, naming, comment density — asked for at install time. The critic grades against stated conventions, never generic taste, at warn/note severity; convention lines face the same only-ever-notes pruning.

skills.name: daily battery
skills.detail: Stays as the backstop and as suk's post-merge validator (D-L4: shoulder mode runs beside it, not instead of it). The nightly LLM pass is repurposed from 're-run green tests' to 'critique what changed since my last run' — role.md:116 already builds a git log --since='24 hours ago' -p block to extend. The measured failure mode it replaces: every recorded failure was environmental (playwright errors, db locks, dirty worktrees), zero real regressions caught, fixAttempts: 0 in every sampled result.json.

skills.name: coverage-audit
skills.source: see mentat — same shipyard/skills file, second consumer
skills.detail: The audit's categorized failure classes (bugs users reported that tests missed) are exactly the classes the release rubric should check for that project. Shared skill, two consumers: mentat proposes work from it, proctor tightens its rubric from it.

crew.name: suk
crew.status: Live. Reverts and restarts are logged honestly; a regression now opens an incident-repair proposal (one road into build) rather than an auto-merge side-door. Mitigation stays instant and ungated.
crew.tagline: Answers when the ship is holed: triage, assign repair, restart, revert.
crew.headline: The merge→validate→revert loop never worked — and its notifications claimed 'reverted' regardless.
crew.gate: reverts and restarts are logged honestly (reverted vs revert_failed); timers fire every 10 minutes

skills.name: merge → validate → revert
skills.source: agents/medic/runner.sh — kept as a self-tested function; the auto-merge path that called it was retired by D-L15 (one road into build)
skills.summary: Post-merge validation with an honest revert path.
skills.detail: The confirmed-live bug chain: post-merge validation invoked the release runner without --project (runner.sh:951), which exits 2 before doing any work, so the outcome was always 'fail', so the revert always ran; git revert -m 1 fails on squash merges (no second parent) and the failure was swallowed by || true; the Signal notification at :962 then claimed 'reverted' no matter what. Fixed (1a/1b): pass --project; choose revert shape by commit topology (rev-parse MERGE_SHA^2); capture the exit code; notify reverted vs revert_failed truthfully; emit medic.action.revert with outcome. Proven against a bats fixture with a real bare-origin + clone topology, both commit shapes, each test first shown failing against the pre-fix code.

skills.name: incident triage
skills.source: agents/medic/runner.sh + check-examples/ — now emits an incident-repair proposal on a code fix
skills.summary: Every-10-minute scan; opens incidents, assigns repair.
skills.detail: Detects failures on live services every 10 minutes. Mitigation is immediate and ungated: restart, revert, contain. Repair is not: on opening an incident, suk emits an incident-repair proposal to the top of the loop (design → stamp → build → release) and pages the operator — it no longer hands fix work directly to build (D-L15). The 10-minute cadence is also the merge-is-live hazard: units execute runners from the dev clone, so anything merged to trunk is live within 10 minutes — all ticket work stays on a branch until the single Phase-4 cutover.

skills.name: --check-config + --self-test
skills.summary: Prove the gates and the loop without touching anything live.
skills.detail: --check-config: read-only effective-gate printout (can_merge, allow_no_ci, resolved trunk, scope globs, budgets) as one JSON object — swept across the whole fleet and diffed against the audited inventory before any cutover. --self-test: run merge → post-merge-fail → revert on the fixture topology for both commit shapes; non-zero exit if the revert path doesn't actually revert.

crew.name: chronicler
crew.status: Live under the new name; its record meets the human operator in the Daily Dispatch.
crew.tagline: Keeps the log: daily doc refresh, the record of what the fleet did.
crew.headline: The only agent whose job was already honest — it gets the rename and nothing else.
crew.gate: docs follow the code; the daily dispatch (hub-side) is where its record meets the human

skills.name: doc refresh
skills.detail: Unchanged behavior; rename-only changes in Phase 9 (unit names, svc strings, role: field on events). Two projects run scribe-only installs today (sietch and the citadel hub itself).

skills.name: install playbook
skills.source: hub skills/install-quartet stubbed → shipyard/skills/install (adds --theme + conventions interview)
skills.summary: The installer keeps the recon, gains the theme knob.
skills.detail: Not chronicler's skill — listed here as the system-level entry. Kept from install-quartet: Step-0 recon (stack detection so a Python project never inherits Node test commands, git remote + gh auth checks, tracked-secrets sweep before anything pushes, medic service-surface and chat-DB shape questions), coverage-audit-first ordering, and the safety sweep. Added: the --theme question (spacetime / plain / custom), role-ID config keys with QUARTET_* accepted through the deprecation window (D-O6). The hub copy becomes a stub.

phases.title: bats test harness
phases.gist: First tests shipyard has ever had: PATH-shim stubs (gh/git/claude/systemctl), fixture configs, a real bare-origin git topology for both merge shapes, wired into CI.

phases.title: Gap fixes 1a–1g
phases.gist: Post-merge --project bug, honest revert by commit shape, merge default → opt-in, no_checks → red, shared trunk detection that never silently defaults, --check-config, medic --self-test. Every fix behind a test first shown failing.

phases.title: salusa CI
phases.gist: The hot case: auto-merge on + zero CI workflows. vitest (1558 tests) + typecheck on PR and push; must land before the gates that would otherwise disable its auto-merge.

phases.title: fleet dry run
phases.gist: Read-only --check-config sweep across all projects from a worktree (the dev clone is live — medic fires every 10 min); any mismatch with the audited inventory blocks the merge.

phases.title: live cutover
phases.gist: The single merge to main. Supervised runs on all four full-install projects; event stream watched for upgrade-caused incidents; rollback sha recorded first.

phases.title: 7-day soak
phases.gist: Failure rate must hold ≤ 8.8% baseline; zero dishonest revert notifications (a truthful revert_failed passes; a false 'reverted' fails).

phases.title: shoulder mode
phases.gist: Proctor's critic built + piloted on caladan: planted-bug test, debounce test, blocking-gate test, cost guard, wrote-no-code check. Hooks merged additively into existing settings.

phases.title: dispatch surfacing
phases.gist: Critique events into the dashboard (timeline detail + a guardians panel that now matches by role across all four projects, old + new svc names) and the Daily Dispatch, verified at mobile viewport.

phases.title: end-to-end gate
phases.gist: Every gate re-run from clean state; 2-week findings measure opens — the human operator judges ≥⅓ actionable, noisy rubric lines pruned.

phases.title: rename + themes
phases.gist: Renamed to shipyard locally (GitHub already renamed); canonical role IDs in code + events; --theme spacetime|plain|custom; fleet re-baked; legacy keys warn for one release.

phases.title: skills-parity
phases.gist: polish-ticket, execute-ticket, coverage-audit adapted into shipyard/skills/; install-quartet replaced; symlinked per-project so agents and humans load identical files.

phases.title: mentat
phases.gist: Collectors + pilot beacons + nightly proposals → dispatch Approve/Deny → polish-ticket → helldiver. Deny-suppression and budget caps proven with bats.

phases.title: 5-day fleet trial
phases.gist: T1 ≥1 real bug caught+fixed · T2 usage assessed for ≥3 projects · T3 ≥1 feature shipped end-to-end · T4 ≥1 consequential decision surfaced and decided. Missed floors become written findings.

phases.title: human front doors
phases.gist: /bugfix and /feature — the human-initiated siblings of mentat's machine-drafted proposals. /bugfix reproduces + root-causes before writing; /feature clarifies the ask + sets a definition of done. Both call /write-ticket (the precursor polish-ticket assumes) → polish → execute → proctor.

install.intro: A harness ships in layers — generic core at the bottom, operator taste at the top. Installing is writing the top layers; upgrading is pulling the bottom one.

layers.name: core
layers.what: Runners, shared lib, skills, installer — generic, zero personal facts (leak-checked in CI). Units execute from the clone: a merge to trunk is fleet-live at the next timer fire.

layers.name: hub knobs
layers.where: baked into systemd units at install time
layers.what: Where events, notifications, and dashboard state flow (env knobs). Changing them means re-running the installer — units bake env, they don't read it live.

layers.name: theme
layers.what: Display names for units, svc strings, and notification voice: spacetime, plain, or five custom names. Role IDs underneath never change.

layers.name: project config
layers.what: The gates and budgets: can_merge (default no), allow_no_ci (default no), trunk branch, token caps (1M/day per agent), test/typecheck commands, paths.

layers.name: roles + conventions
layers.what: Project-specific prompt extensions per crew role, and the ## Conventions block — the operator's stated taste (LOC economy, dependency policy, naming) that the release critic grades against.

layers.name: skills
layers.what: polish-ticket, execute-ticket, coverage-audit — the same files agents load headless and humans invoke in-session.

install.flow: Install = recon first (coverage-audit reads the project's session history before anything is configured) → interview (theme, gates, service surface, conventions) → write layers 3–5 → bake units → verify timers fire. Uninstall = remove units + delete .agents/ — the repo keeps nothing it didn't choose.

adaptation.intro: Adaptation is versioned edits to the files agents and humans share — every lesson routed to the layer where it belongs, inherited by every future caller.

channels.name: operator corrections → skill files
channels.how: When the operator corrects a behavior, the correction lands as an edit to the skill, rubric, or ticket — reviewable, revertable, inherited. From this system's own history: polish-ticket's 'traps that have bitten this box' section is accreted incident history (a stale dashboard bundle that made shipped changes look absent; a runaway headless browser that hammered an API for 1d 17h), and execute-ticket's honest-blocker protocol exists because sessions faked green. Burned session → line in the skill file → no caller repeats it.

channels.name: coverage-audit → rubric + proposals
channels.how: Session transcripts are mined for bugs the operator reported that tests missed. Each miss is triaged: project-specific (→ .agents/<role>.md), generic (→ core role files, upstream PR), or install-time (→ a new installer question). The audit taxonomy IS the adaptation router.

channels.name: user feedback → build
channels.how: Asynchronous feedback (chat notes, fyi-requests.jsonl) is triaged nightly by the build crew into PRs; substantial asks become design proposals instead of drive-by patches.

channels.name: agent → agent
channels.how: Proctor's findings drop into the working agent's live session as notes (never hard stops); suk's incidents become mentat's design signal at the top of the loop; execute-ticket's ledger notes feed the next polish pass. Agents correct each other through the same reviewable surfaces humans use.

channels.name: pruning
channels.how: Rubric and convention lines that only ever produce notes for two weeks are removed. Adaptation includes forgetting — a critic that flags everything teaches nothing.

adaptation.router: The routing rule, stated once: one-off correction → the ticket's decision tables; project taste → .agents/<role>.md Conventions; project gates → config.toml; portable doctrine → a core PR (leak-checked, fleet-live on merge); a question every future install should ask → the installer interview.

graph.note: Every agent inherits a subset of one shared skill set. The spine write-ticket → polish-ticket → execute-ticket is the pipeline both a human and an agent run; /bugfix and /feature are the human front doors into it; coverage-audit and check-config span several agents. Dotted membership lines show the overlap.

anti-cheating brief.def: The clause handed verbatim to every delegated subagent before it starts work.
anti-cheating brief.example:
> Converge honestly or report the precise blocker with the actual evidence —
> NEVER fake green, weaken a check, or hand-wave "should work". Run the real
> command, read the real file, curl the real port, and report exact output
> (exit codes, JSONL lines, HTTP codes), not adjectives.

per-phase gate assembly.def: Each ticket phase names which gate classes apply to it and their exact commands, drawn from the project's gate file — no blanket test command.
per-phase gate assembly.example:
The ticket must state, for EVERY phase, which gate classes from
.agents/gates.md apply and the exact commands. "The code looks right" is never
proof. The standard menu the gate file draws from:
- Shell: `bash -n` on every touched script; then run it for real and
  read the output. Scripts with a `--check`/`--dry-run` flag: run that too.
- systemd (user) changes: `systemctl --user daemon-reload`, then
  `list-timers` shows the expected next fire …

merge-is-live.def: The systemd timers run the runners straight from the dev clone, so a merge to trunk is live across the fleet within one timer tick.
merge-is-live.example:
merge-is-live: systemd units run runners from this dev clone; a merge to
`main` is fleet-live at the next timer fire, and medic/suk timers fire
every 10 min — work on a branch until tested, and CI only triggers on a
PR (not a branch push)
merge-is-live.source: a live install's .agents/gates.md — cross-repo gate table

skills-parity.def: Headless agents and in-session humans load the exact same skill files — one definition of each workflow, no drifting pair.
skills-parity.example:
**Where:** `<project>/.claude/skills/` (symlinks into `<harness>/skills/`).
**What:** `write-ticket`, `bug`, `feature`, `polish-ticket`, `execute-ticket`,
`coverage-audit` — **the same files agents load headless and humans invoke
in-session.** One implementation, two callers, no agent-only fork.

stamp.def: The human approve/deny decision in the daily dispatch — the one required human gate before a proposal becomes buildable work.
stamp.source: data/news/decisions.jsonl — the live dispatch ledger (today's entries)

cold-context critique.def: A review that sees only the diff, the changed-file list, and the rubric — never the authoring session — so the author's goal can't contaminate it.
cold-context critique.example:
{"ts":"2026-07-21T23:57:09Z","svc":"caladan-guardian","event":"guardian.critique",
 "source":"shoulder","files":2,"block":1,"warn":3,"note":1,"tokens":1764}
→ the block: "any unauthenticated caller can delete any trip's conversation" —
  a planted auth bug, caught from the diff alone.
cold-context critique.source: the live event stream (a real critique event, 2026-07-21) + its finding

the gate file.def: A project's .agents/gates.md — the single source of truth for how to prove a change works on that project.
the gate file.example:
> The single source of truth for **how to prove a change works on this
> project**. `polish-ticket` reads this file to assemble the per-phase
> verification surface; `execute-ticket` reads it to know which gates to run
> before every commit. There is no one test command that covers everything —
> a phase declares which of the classes below apply, and this file supplies
> the exact commands.
the gate file.source: skills/gates.md.template:3 — dropped at install, never clobbered

--check-config.def: A read-only dry run that prints a project's fully-resolved configuration, so the whole fleet can be swept for drift without running anything.

--self-test.def: The on-call runner proves its own merge → post-merge-fail → revert recovery path on a throwaway git fixture, for both commit shapes.
--self-test.example:
$ bash agents/medic/runner.sh --self-test
self-test: squash — merge -> post-merge-fail -> revert OK
self-test: true-merge — merge -> post-merge-fail -> revert OK
self-test: PASS (shapes exercised: squash, true-merge)

north star.def: A repo's one-line compass — its top-line GitHub description — read by the design loop as a directional prior when drafting proposals.
north star.example:
$ gh repo view wabbazzar/shipyard --json description -q .description
Ship real improvements at agent speed — every proposal from live evidence,
every merge behind an unfakeable gate, exactly one human stamp in the loop.
north star.source: real output — shipyard's own live GitHub description

A–H checklist.def: The eight-section hardening checklist polish-ticket runs a ticket through before it is safe to build unattended.
A–H checklist.example:
### A. Self-containment
### B. Orchestration protocol
### C. The gate (make it inescapable) — assembled per phase from the gate file
### D. System discipline — traps; pin the relevant ones
### E. Verification surface (the heart of it)
### F. Decisions
### G. Phasing
### H. Ledger + Definition of Done

self-containment.def: Checklist item A: the ticket must name everything exactly so a zero-context agent never has to guess.
self-containment.example:
### A. Self-containment
A zero-context agent must not have to guess. Name exact files with paths
(`repo:file:line` where it matters), exact unit/timer names, exact ports,
exact config keys. Cross-repo work names the target repo, branch strategy,
and that repo's own gate. Numbers cited from live state (event counts, config
values) get a **date and the command that produced them** — the system drifts.

orchestration protocol.def: Checklist item B: the builder acts as an orchestrator that delegates heavy work to subagents but re-verifies everything itself.
orchestration protocol.example:
### B. Orchestration protocol
State that the builder is an orchestrator: delegate heavy/wide work to
subagents with tight briefs, keep the orchestrator lean, re-verify personally.
Embed the verbatim anti-cheating brief:

toolchain proof.def: Checklist item C2: the polisher must have personally run every verification tool the ticket relies on before handing it over.
toolchain proof.example:
### C2. VERIFY THE TICKET'S OWN TOOLCHAIN EXISTS
Do not hand over a ticket whose verification method you have not run at least
once **while polishing**. If it calls for bats, shellcheck, a browser, gh, an
API token — prove it works on this box now and record the exact invocation
(and any install fix) in the ticket. Where you can, replace adjectives with
**measured baseline numbers** taken during polishing (event-stream counts, …

verification surface.def: The per-behavior proof plan: for each thing the ticket adds, exactly which command to run and what it must show.
verification surface.example:
### E. Verification surface (the heart of it)
For EACH behavior the ticket adds, write down exactly how to prove it:
which command to run, which file/port/JSONL line to read, what it must show,
and — where a test harness exists or the ticket creates one — the **specific
test case to add, which must be able to FAIL on the real defect** (a test
that can't fail is a finding, not a test). Prefer deterministic checks;
anything time/timer-dependent gets an explicit wait-or-trigger instruction …

thin vertical slices.def: The phasing rule: each phase is a small, independently verifiable, single-commit slice that never leaves the live system broken.
thin vertical slices.example:
### G. Phasing
Thin vertical slices, each independently verifiable and a single clean
commit, ordered so **the live system is never left broken between phases**
(a half-migrated unit, a stopped service, an unbaked env). Cross-repo tickets
sequence sibling-repo phases before the hub phases that depend on them. No
big-bang phase. A final phase re-runs the whole ticket's gate end-to-end.

ledger.def: A running section in the ticket where the builder records its plan, commit hash, and honest notes for every phase.
ledger.example:
### H. Ledger + Definition of Done
Give the ticket a **Ledger** section (builder appends plan + commit hash per
phase, honest notes on anything deferred) and an observable DoD per phase —
stated as "this command shows X / this JSONL line appears / this port
returns 200," not "implemented." …

honest-blocker protocol.def: Execute-ticket's rule for when a phase can't be made green: stop, revert to the last green state, and report precisely — never fake it.
honest-blocker protocol.example:
If a phase can't be made green by legitimate means: STOP iterating. Do NOT
fake green, weaken a check, loosen a gate, or leave a unit disabled to dodge
a failure. Diagnose with evidence — exactly what fails, the actual output,
why the approach can't work. If it needs a user decision → §4. If it's
genuinely bigger than the ticket assumed → report it, revert to the last
GREEN state (preserve work as a patch + note where), record the honest state
in the Ledger. A precise "here's what's left and why" beats a faked-green
tree every time. This is the single most valuable behavior in the skill.

user-decision class.def: The only category of question an autonomous build may stop for — decisions that genuinely belong to the owner.
user-decision class.example:
Autonomous by default. Stop and ASK only when the decision is genuinely the
owner's: (a) **spending money**; (b) **outward-facing or public actions**
(DNS, newly exposed ports/services, publishing, messaging anyone who isn't
the owner); (c) **destructive or hard-to-reverse system changes** (deleting
data, firewall edits beyond the ticket's pre-approved ones, disabling
services other things depend on); (d) **overriding a deliberately-configured
live behavior** (a config value whose comment shows intent); (e) **a real
design fork with no sensible default** where guessing wrong burns large …

shoulder mode.def: The release critic running concurrently beside the authoring session, judging the diff cold while the code is still being written.
shoulder mode.example:
You are **guardian's shoulder-mode critic**. You review a diff the way a
release engineer would: cold, with no attachment to the goal that
produced it. You NEVER write code — you critique. Another agent (or a
human) decides what to do with your findings.

debounce window.def: The batching rule that decides when the shoulder-mode critic actually fires instead of critiquing every keystroke.
debounce window.example:
# Trigger: (queue idle >= CRITIC_IDLE_SEC AND non-empty)
#          OR (>= CRITIC_BATCH_FILES distinct files queued).
# Env knobs (test overrides): CRITIC_IDLE_SEC (300), CRITIC_BATCH_FILES (8),
# CRITIC_POLL_SEC (30), GUARDIAN_CRITIC_MODEL (claude default model if unset).

event stream.def: The append-only JSONL log, one file per day, where every agent run and incident lands as a machine-readable line.

fyi-requests.jsonl.def: A per-project append-only file of asynchronous user feedback that the build crew triages nightly into PRs.
fyi-requests.jsonl.example:
{"ts":"2026-06-04T19:21:18.000Z","id":"fyi_20260604192118_k952c","username":"operator", …
 "text":"The badge icons look good — nice work on those."}

Conventions block.def: A section of the project's role file holding the operator's stated taste, which the release critic grades against instead of generic style opinions.
Conventions block.example:
## Conventions layer

If the project's `.agents/release.md` contains a `## Conventions`
block, grade the diff against those STATED conventions only — never
against generic taste. Convention findings are capped at `warn`/`note`
severity; a convention miss is never a `block` on its own.

daily dispatch.def: The daily machine-written newspaper where the fleet's overnight activity meets the human — including the Approve/Deny queue where proposals get stamped.
daily dispatch.example:
"edition": 203,
"title": "The Citadel Dispatch",
"motto": "All the agents, fit to print",
…
"kicker": "MEDIC-INCIDENT · ATTENTION",
"headline": "Salusa Guardian Falters; Prod Restart Pending as 4xx Surge Hits"

one road into build.def: The D-L15 rule that a code fix — even for an incident — may only enter the build loop as a stamped proposal, never through a side-door.
one road into build.example:
# D-L15 — incident repair no longer escalates to build/augur (that
      # side-door is retired). Medic writes an IMMEDIATE incident-repair
      # proposal into the design loop's result file (mentat → helldiver via
      # the dispatch), pages once, and lets the owner stamp it. This is a
      # deterministic write: no augur.lock, no model spend, no merge. The
      # restart/revert MITIGATION classes above are untouched — suk still
      # restarts/reverts immediately; only the CODE FIX is rerouted.

traps that have bitten this box.def: The append-only incident-history appendix in a project's gate file — each burned session becomes a line no future caller repeats.
traps that have bitten this box.example:
## Traps that have bitten this project  (append-only)

The accreted incident history — every burned session becomes a line here so no
future caller repeats it. This is the hub's **learning surface**.

- **Stale dashboard bundle** — edited `dashboard/src/**`, forgot `npm run build
  && systemctl --user restart dashboard`, then stared at an unchanged page
  convinced the edit didn't take. Rebuild+restart is mandatory, no "too small …

Goodhart check.def: The critic's first rubric line: did the change actually do what the task claims, or just satisfy the specific eval the author optimized against?
Goodhart check.example:
1. **Goodhart check** — does the change do what the task claims,
   beyond the specific eval/test the author optimized? A fix that
   special-cases the failing input is a `block`.
